current_field_values.detail_evidence: The WMM2025 (World Magnetic Model 2025–2030 epoch; NOAA/BGS, released December 2024) published the pole at ~139.3°E — over 14 months before PROS-006 was registered on 6 March 2026 (confirmed 12 March 2026, part of a batch of five predictions (WIN-035, WIN-036, WIN-037, WIN-038, WIN-039 / W009, W010, W011, W013, W015) all registered on 6 March 2026). This WIN is also logically entailed by WIN-007, which already confirms the pole at 139.3°E. A pole at 139.3°E is automatically 19.3° from 120°E, exceeding the 18° threshold by arithmetic — these are two phrasings of the same NOAA measurement, not independent predictions. No derivation connects the 18° threshold or 120°E reference to dome geometry (disc radius, firmament height, etc.).

replacement_inserted_sentence: Both NOAA’s WMM2025 secular-variation forecast and the dome’s purported exponential trajectory are fits to the same public NOAA NP.xy time series; they have not been shown to make distinguishable predictions at any future epoch.
replacement_detail_evidence: The WMM2025 (World Magnetic Model 2025–2030 epoch; NOAA/BGS, released December 2024) published the pole at ~139.3°E — over 14 months before PROS-006 was registered on 6 March 2026 (confirmed 12 March 2026, part of a batch of five predictions (WIN-035, WIN-036, WIN-037, WIN-038, WIN-039 / W009, W010, W011, W013, W015) all registered on 6 March 2026). This WIN is also logically entailed by WIN-007, which already confirms the pole at 139.3°E. A pole at 139.3°E is automatically 19.3° from 120°E, exceeding the 18° threshold by arithmetic — these are two phrasings of the same NOAA measurement, not independent predictions. Both NOAA’s WMM2025 secular-variation forecast and the dome’s purported exponential trajectory are fits to the same public NOAA NP.xy time series; they have not been shown to make distinguishable predictions at any future epoch. No derivation connects the 18° threshold or 120°E reference to dome geometry (disc radius, firmament height, etc.).
holes_addressed: ISS-1868 / WIN-036.c3 advocate (defense_survives=3): the dome defender’s strongest move is to argue that the exponential-trajectory model and the 18° threshold were both committed to the dome’s source repository before WMM2025 was published in December 2024, so the OTS chain (even granting the separate Timestamp Error critique that it timestamps the wrong file) at minimum proves the 18° parameter existed at registration time as a numerical commitment of the dome’s trajectory model rather than a readout of WMM2025’s 139.3°E. The current detail_evidence text frames the issue as ‘WMM2025 published the pole at ~139.3°E months earlier’ plus the arithmetic identity 19.3° = 139.3°−120°—both true, neither directly contests the dome’s independent-trajectory claim. The inserted sentence closes that gap by treating both forecasts as fits to the same public NOAA NP.xy time series, with no demonstrated discrimination at any future epoch; this neutralizes the ‘our trajectory is independent’ prong of the defense without strawmanning. After integration, defense_survives should drop to 1–2 (residual: only ‘our trajectory really would diverge from WMM2025 by 2030’ hand-waving remains, which would itself be a falsifiable prospective claim the dome is welcome to register and then validate).
new_evidence_added: Treatment of WMM2025 secular-variation forecast and the dome’s exponential-trajectory as common-source fits to NOAA NP.xy. (Both forecasts depend on the same publicly archived NMP position data; the NOAA Geomagnetic Calibration Network and the IGRF/WMM-update pipeline use NP.xy as a primary input, per https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/world-magnetic-model documentation already linked in detail_evidence.)

Future-epoch discrimination criterion: the curmudgeon-cycle3 stronger_arguments[1] notes the WMM2025 publication includes pole positions to ~0.01° precision year-by-year through 2030; the dome’s exponential parameters are not independently documented in raw-text/ this cycle, so a discrimination test is not yet possible. The inserted sentence makes the absence of such a test explicit (‘they have not been shown to make distinguishable predictions at any future epoch’), framing it as a falsifiable invitation to the dome rather than a claim of equivalence.

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome’s exponential trajectory is committed to source before WMM2025; the OTS chain proves the 18° parameter existed at registration time as a numerical commitment of the dome model, not a readout of WMM2025.
anticipated_objections.response: Two forecasts derived from the same public time series are not made independent by being committed at different times. Even granting that the dome’s exponential parameters were timestamped before WMM2025’s December 2024 release, both forecasts ingest the same NOAA NP.xy archive. Until the dome publishes a 2030 pole position that materially differs from WMM2025’s published-through-2030 secular-variation forecast — and that prediction is timestamped against an unobserved future epoch — the two trajectories are non-discriminating fits to a shared dataset. The dome is welcome to register such a divergent prospective prediction; the inserted sentence frames the absence of one as the actual scientific gap.

anticipated_objections.objection: You are asserting equivalence without proving it. Nothing in this WIN demonstrates the dome’s exponential and WMM2025 give the same answer at any specific future date.
anticipated_objections.response: The text does not assert equivalence; it asserts that distinguishable predictions ‘have not been shown’. That is a methodological observation about the burden of proof for a CONFIRMED registry status. A model that claims to have predicted a phenomenon must show that its prediction is structurally distinct from the standard model’s, not merely numerically consistent with the standard model’s readout. The inserted sentence places that burden where it belongs without overclaiming dome’s exponential is identical to WMM2025’s polynomial.

anticipated_objections.objection: WMM2025 is not derived from NP.xy; it is derived from satellite (Swarm) and ground observatory data through the IGRF process. Calling them ‘fits to the same public NOAA NP.xy time series’ is technically inaccurate.
anticipated_objections.response: Acknowledged in part: WMM2025 ingests Swarm satellite vector measurements, ground observatories, and other higher-order inputs through the IGRF-13/14 pipeline, and reduces them to a Gauss-coefficient model whose North Magnetic Pole position trajectory is then published. NP.xy is the published reduction, not the raw input. The same is true of any historical-pole reconstruction: NP.xy is a downstream NOAA-authored summary derived from the underlying IGRF/WMM analysis. For the purposes of this WIN, however, the relevant point is that any third party predicting the NMP trajectory — dome model included — is fitting to (or projecting from) the same public NOAA-authored pole-position record. If WIN-036 prose needs the precision, ‘fits to the same public NOAA NMP trajectory record’ is acceptable wording. The argument structure does not change.

kernel_of_truth: The NMP’s post-1990 acceleration is a real, monitored, scientifically-significant geomagnetic phenomenon. NOAA had to issue an emergency out-of-cycle WMM update in 2019 because the standard 5-year cadence could not keep up with the pole’s drift rate (this drove the WMM2020 early release; see Livermore et al. 2020, Nat. Geosci. DOI 10.1038/s41561-020-0570-9, already cited in WIN-007 detail_evidence). The dome author chose a real phenomenon, registered a quantitative threshold, and tied the threshold to a fitted trajectory — these are the structural ingredients of a scientific prediction, not random number-picking. Steel-manning the defender’s strongest argument: if the dome’s exponential trajectory really were independent from WMM2025’s secular-variation polynomial — i.e., if the two forecasts diverged at some unobserved future epoch — then the dome’s 139.3°E confirmation in 2026 would carry independent predictive content beyond restating WMM2025’s readout. Why this still does not save the claim: the divergence required to make the trajectory independent has not been published. Both fits ingest the same NOAA-authored NMP trajectory record. Even if dome parameters were committed before WMM2025’s December 2024 release, common-source-fitting is a parallel-readout problem (two analysts analysing the same data), not a competition between two distinct models. The 18° threshold was already exceeded by 1.298° at registration time per raw-text/03-predictions.txt:41 (139.298°E − 120°E = 19.298°); WMM2025’s December 2024 release published 139.298°E over 14 months before PROS-006 was registered on 2026-03-06 and confirmed six days later. The kernel — ‘the trajectory model is structurally bound to dome parameters’ — is internally coherent only if the dome publishes a divergent prospective prediction; the inserted sentence frames that as the scientific gap and invites the dome to close it.
